schools.summary: God appears as El Shaddai: 'and when Abram was ninety years old and nine, the LORD appeared to Abram, and said unto him, I am the Almighty God; walk before me, and be thou perfect' — God appearing to Abram (thirteen years after Ishmael's birth, 16:16) and revealing himself by a new name, El Shaddai, 'God Almighty' (or 'the all-sufficient God,' the name marking God's omnipotence and all-sufficiency, fit to assure Abram, now ninety-nine, with a barren wife, that God could fulfill the promise of a son against all impossibility, the God almighty to do what he had promised); 'El Shaddai' (variously 'Almighty,' or 'the sufficient/all-bountiful One') declaring God's power and sufficiency (the name that strengthened Abram's faith — the God who is almighty and all-sufficient could give the promised seed and fulfill the covenant, the ground of faith in the omnipotent God); 'walk before me, and be thou perfect (upright, sincere, blameless)' (God calling Abram to walk before him, in his presence, in faith and obedience, and to be perfect — not sinlessly perfect, but upright, sincere, and wholehearted, the duty of the covenant — to walk before the all-sufficient God in integrity, the response of faith and holiness to the God who reveals himself as Almighty) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Henry.

themes: And when Abram was ninety years old and nine, the LORD appeared to Abram, and said unto him, I am the Almighty God; walk before me, and be thou perfect
God appearing as the Almighty (El Shaddai) and calling Abram to walk before him
the omnipotent, all-sufficient God, able to fulfill the promise
the call to upright, sincere, wholehearted obedience

schools.summary: God's covenant and the multiplying: 'and I will make my covenant between me and thee, and will multiply thee exceedingly' — God declaring he would make (establish, confirm) his covenant with Abram, and multiply him exceedingly (the renewal and enlarging of the covenant, 15:18, the promise of a numerous posterity, now to be sealed with the sign of circumcision, vv.10-11); God to 'make my covenant' (establish his gracious engagement with Abram, the covenant of grace, by which God bound himself to be Abram's God and to give him the seed and the land, the solemn confirming of the covenant); 'will multiply thee exceedingly' (the promise of an exceedingly numerous posterity, the great nation, 12:2; the seed as the stars, 15:5, the multiplying of Abram into many nations, v.4, the covenant blessing of fruitfulness, the assurance, to the childless old man, of a vast seed by God's almighty power, v.1) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And I will make my covenant between me and thee, and will multiply thee exceedingly
God's promise to establish his covenant and multiply Abram
the gracious confirming of the covenant (15:18)
the promise of a vast posterity, by the power of the Almighty (v.1)

schools.summary: Abram falls on his face: 'and Abram fell on his face: and God talked with him, saying' — Abram, before the appearing of the Almighty (v.1), falling on his face — the posture of profound reverence, awe, and worship (the patriarch prostrate before God, the humility and adoration of Abram in the divine presence, God continuing to talk with him, vv.4-21); Abram falling on his face — in reverence and humility before the Almighty (the gesture of worship and submission, the awe of the creature before the Creator, the patriarch's adoration of the God who appeared and spoke); 'God talked with him' (God graciously continuing to speak with Abram, the prostrate patriarch, the condescension of God to commune with his servant, the familiar dealing of God with Abram, the friend of God, James 2:23; 2 Chronicles 20:7, the revelation of the covenant to the worshiping patriarch) — Gill, Clarke, Wesley, Calvin.

themes: And Abram fell on his face: and God talked with him, saying
Abram falling on his face before God
the posture of profound reverence, awe, and worship
God's condescension to commune with his servant (James 2:23)

schools.summary: Abram a father of many nations: 'as for me, behold, my covenant is with thee, and thou shalt be a father of many nations' — God declaring his part in the covenant: his gracious engagement with Abram ('as for me' marking God's side of the covenant, Abram's side being to keep it, v.9, the LORD binding himself to Abram by covenant); 'thou shalt be a father of many nations' (Abram the father of many nations: not Israel only, but many peoples, the Ishmaelites, the children of Keturah, the Edomites, and, spiritually, all the faithful of every nation, Romans 4:11-12, 16-18, the father of all who believe, the wide fulfillment of the promise, Abram the father of a multitude); the promise embracing both Abram's natural descendants (the many nations of his seed) and his spiritual children (the believing Gentiles, who are Abraham's seed by faith, Galatians 3:7, 29, Abram the father of the faithful, the new name, v.5, sealing this fatherhood of many nations) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: As for me, behold, my covenant is with thee, and thou shalt be a father of many nations
the promise that Abram shall be a father of many nations
the father of many peoples, natural and spiritual (Romans 4:11-18)
the father of all who believe, of every nation (Galatians 3:7, 29)

schools.summary: Abram renamed Abraham: 'neither shall thy name any more be called Abram, but thy name shall be Abraham; for a father of many nations have I made thee' — God changing Abram's name (Abram, 'high/exalted father') to Abraham ('father of a multitude,' the new name, given by God, marking and sealing the covenant promise, the change of name a token of the new relation and the new promise, the patriarch renamed for his destiny); 'Abraham' meaning 'father of a multitude' (ab-hamon, father of many, the name embodying the promise, v.4, 'have I made thee,' God speaking of it as already done, the certainty of the promise, Abraham, the father of many nations, by God's appointment, the name a perpetual witness of the covenant); the changing of the name (as later Sarai to Sarah, v.15, and Jacob to Israel, 32:28) marking a new stage in God's dealing and a new identity under the covenant (the name Abraham a standing memorial of the promise, the patriarch bearing in his very name the assurance that he would be the father of many nations, the seal of the covenant in the new name) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott.

themes: Neither shall thy name any more be called Abram, but thy name shall be Abraham; for a father of many nations have I made thee
the changing of Abram's name to Abraham
Abraham, 'father of a multitude,' the name embodying the promise
the new name a standing memorial and seal of the covenant

schools.summary: Fruitfulness, nations, and kings: 'and I will make thee exceeding fruitful, and I will make nations of thee, and kings shall come out of thee' — God promising Abraham exceeding fruitfulness (a vast posterity), nations (many peoples descending from him), and kings (royal descendants, the enlarging of the promise, the patriarch the source of nations and kings); 'nations' coming of Abraham — Israel, and the other peoples of his seed (Ishmaelites, Edomites, the sons of Keturah, the many nations descending from the one man, the fulfillment of the name Abraham, v.5); 'kings shall come out of thee' (kings descending from Abraham, the kings of Israel and Judah, of Edom, and others, and supremely the King, the Messiah, Christ, of Abraham's line, Matthew 1:1, the royal promise, the patriarch the ancestor of kings, and of the King of kings, the dignity of Abraham's seed) — Gill.

themes: And I will make thee exceeding fruitful, and I will make nations of thee, and kings shall come out of thee
the promise of fruitfulness, nations, and kings from Abraham
the patriarch the source of many nations and kings
supremely the King, the Messiah, of Abraham's line (Matthew 1:1)

schools.summary: The everlasting covenant: 'and I will establish my covenant between me and thee and thy seed after thee in their generations for an everlasting covenant, to be a God unto thee, and to thy seed after thee' — God establishing his covenant with Abraham and his seed throughout their generations, as an everlasting covenant (the covenant perpetual, enduring, in its substance the covenant of grace, to all generations and forever, the unbreakable, eternal engagement of God); the substance of the covenant 'to be a God unto thee, and to thy seed after thee' (God himself, given to Abraham and his seed as their God, the highest blessing — that the LORD would be their God, their portion, their salvation, the covenant relation, I will be their God, and they shall be my people, Jeremiah 31:33, the formula of the covenant of grace, the heart of all the promises — God himself the believer's God); the everlasting covenant, 'to be a God' to Abraham and his seed, the covenant of grace, fulfilled in Christ and extending to all the faithful (Abraham's spiritual seed, Galatians 3:29, God's gracious, perpetual engagement to be the God of his people, the foundation of the believer's hope and salvation, the covenant that secures God himself as the portion of his people forever) — Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And I will establish my covenant... for an everlasting covenant, to be a God unto thee, and to thy seed after thee
the everlasting covenant, 'to be a God unto thee and to thy seed'
God himself given to Abraham and his seed as their God, the heart of the covenant
the perpetual covenant of grace, fulfilled in Christ (Galatians 3:29)

schools.summary: The land for an everlasting possession: 'and I will give unto thee, and to thy seed after thee, the land wherein thou art a stranger, all the land of Canaan, for an everlasting possession; and I will be their God' — God granting the land of Canaan (in which Abraham was now a stranger and sojourner, Hebrews 11:9, possessing none of it, Acts 7:5) to Abraham and his seed (the land-promise, 12:7; 15:18, renewed and confirmed, the inheritance assured to his descendants); the land given 'for an everlasting possession' (the perpetual grant of Canaan to Abraham's seed, Israel, for the appointed age, and, typologically, the heavenly inheritance, the everlasting rest, Hebrews 11:10, 16, of which Canaan was a type, the land the everlasting possession of the covenant-people); 'I will be their God' (the repeated heart of the covenant, v.7 — God himself the God of Abraham's seed, the land-promise sealed with the covenant relation, the inheritance of the land joined to the inheritance of God himself, the covenant of grace, God the portion of his people) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Henry.

themes: And I will give unto thee, and to thy seed after thee, the land wherein thou art a stranger, all the land of Canaan, for an everlasting possession; and I will be their God
the grant of the land of Canaan for an everlasting possession
the land (a type of the heavenly inheritance, Hebrews 11:10) granted to Abraham's seed
the land-promise joined to the gift of God himself as their God

schools.summary: The duty to keep the covenant: 'and God said unto Abraham, Thou shalt keep my covenant therefore, thou, and thy seed after thee in their generations' — having declared his own part (vv.4-8), God now charging Abraham with his part: to keep the covenant (the duty of the covenant-people, to observe and keep God's covenant, and its sign, circumcision, vv.10-14, the response of obedience to God's grace); Abraham and his seed to 'keep my covenant' (to observe its terms, especially the sign of circumcision, vv.10-11, the obligation of the covenant-people, not that the covenant rested on man's keeping, it is God's gracious engagement, but that those in covenant must keep its sign and walk before God, v.1); the duty binding Abraham and his seed throughout their generations (the keeping of the covenant, and circumcision, a perpetual obligation of the covenant-people, the response of faith and obedience to God's everlasting covenant, the covenant kept, generation after generation, in its appointed sign) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And God said unto Abraham, Thou shalt keep my covenant therefore, thou, and thy seed after thee in their generations
the duty to keep the covenant
Abraham's part - the obedience of the covenant-people, especially circumcision
the perpetual obligation to keep the covenant and its sign

schools.summary: The institution of circumcision: 'this is my covenant, which ye shall keep, between me and you and thy seed after thee; Every man child among you shall be circumcised' — God appointing circumcision as the sign and obligation of the covenant ('this is my covenant,' that is, the sign of it, which Abraham's house must keep, the institution of circumcision, the token of the covenant, v.11, to be observed by all the males); every male among Abraham's seed and household to be circumcised (the cutting of the foreskin, v.11, the distinguishing mark of the covenant-people, the rite by which a male was admitted into the covenant of Abraham, the sign borne in the flesh); circumcision, the appointed sign, the seal of the covenant (Romans 4:11) and the badge of the covenant-people (it signifying the covenant relation and, in its spiritual import, v.11, the circumcision of the heart, the sign instituted for Abraham and his seed, the outward token of the inward covenant) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott, Calvin.

themes: This is my covenant, which ye shall keep, between me and you and thy seed after thee; Every man child among you shall be circumcised
the institution of circumcision as the covenant-sign
the distinguishing mark of the covenant-people, borne in the flesh
the sign and seal of the covenant (Romans 4:11)

schools.summary: Calvin and Gill on circumcision the token and seal: 'and ye shall circumcise the flesh of your foreskin; and it shall be a token of the covenant betwixt me and you' — the rite of circumcision, the cutting off of the foreskin (the bodily sign appointed for the covenant-people, the mark borne in the flesh); circumcision 'a token (sign) of the covenant' (the outward sign and seal of the covenant of grace, Paul declaring it 'a seal of the righteousness of the faith' which Abraham had, Romans 4:11 — that is, circumcision sealing and confirming the righteousness Abraham already had by faith, 15:6, before he was circumcised, the sign attesting and ratifying the covenant promise and the righteousness of faith); the sign also signifying, in its spiritual intention, the circumcision of the heart — the putting off of the body of the flesh, the mortifying of sin, regeneration (Deuteronomy 10:16; 30:6; Jeremiah 4:4; Romans 2:28-29; Colossians 2:11, the cutting away of the flesh a figure of the spiritual cleansing and renewal of the heart, the outward sign of an inward grace); circumcision a token, a seal, and a figure — the badge of the covenant-people, the seal of the righteousness of faith, Romans 4:11, and the type of the circumcision of the heart, Colossians 2:11, not a mere fleshly or national mark, but a sign with deep spiritual significance, pointing to the inward grace of regeneration and the righteousness of faith — Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

debates.question: What is the significance of circumcision?

sides.position: The outward sign and seal of the covenant and of the righteousness of faith (Romans 4:11), signifying the circumcision of the heart - regeneration, the putting off of the flesh (Deuteronomy 30:6; Colossians 2:11)
A merely fleshly or national mark, the badge of the covenant-people (the bare external reading, which the apostolic teaching corrects)
holders: the merely-external reading (corrected by Romans 4:11; Colossians 2:11)

themes: And ye shall circumcise the flesh of your foreskin; and it shall be a token of the covenant betwixt me and you
circumcision as the token of the covenant (the crux of its significance)
the seal of the righteousness of faith (Romans 4:11)
signifying the circumcision of the heart, regeneration (Deuteronomy 30:6; Colossians 2:11)

schools.summary: Circumcision on the eighth day: 'and he that is eight days old shall be circumcised among you, every man child in your generations, he that is born in the house, or bought with money of any stranger, which is not of thy seed' — every male to be circumcised on the eighth day after birth (the appointed time for the rite, later the day of Christ's circumcision, Luke 2:21, and of naming, the infant brought into the covenant on the eighth day, the time fixed by God); 'every man child in your generations' to be circumcised (the rite perpetual, binding every male of Abraham's seed throughout the generations, the standing obligation of the covenant-people, the sign borne by every male); the rite extending beyond Abraham's natural seed to 'he that is born in the house, or bought with money of any stranger' (the home-born servants and the purchased slaves, even those not of Abraham's blood, all the males of the household to be circumcised and so brought into the covenant, the sign embracing the whole household of faith, not the natural seed only, a foreshadowing of the Gentiles' incorporation into the covenant) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And he that is eight days old shall be circumcised among you, every man child in your generations, he that is born in the house, or bought with money of any stranger
the circumcision of every male on the eighth day
the rite perpetual, binding every male of Abraham's seed
the sign embracing the whole household, servants and natural seed alike

schools.summary: The covenant in the flesh: 'he that is born in thy house, and he that is bought with thy money, must needs be circumcised: and my covenant shall be in your flesh for an everlasting covenant' — all the males of the household (home-born and purchased) to be circumcised (the rite obligatory for the whole household, the emphatic 'must needs be' marking the necessity, the sign borne by all the males of Abraham's house); the rite embracing both the home-born servants and the bought slaves (the whole household, not the natural seed only, all brought into the covenant by the sign, the embracing of the household of faith); 'my covenant shall be in your flesh for an everlasting covenant' (the covenant-sign borne in the flesh, a perpetual token, circumcision, the abiding mark of the covenant in the body, the everlasting covenant sealed in the flesh of the covenant-people, the sign that would distinguish Abraham's seed, until its fulfillment and abrogation in Christ, Galatians 5:6; Colossians 2:11) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: He that is born in thy house, and he that is bought with thy money, must needs be circumcised: and my covenant shall be in your flesh for an everlasting covenant
that all the household must be circumcised, the covenant in their flesh
the whole household marked with the covenant-sign, not the seed only
the perpetual sign borne in the flesh (until its fulfillment in Christ, Colossians 2:11)

schools.summary: The penalty for neglecting circumcision: 'and the uncircumcised man child whose flesh of his foreskin is not circumcised, that soul shall be cut off from his people; he hath broken my covenant' — the male who was not circumcised (by his own neglect, or by his parents' default, when grown to be answerable) to be 'cut off from his people' (the penalty for despising or neglecting the covenant-sign, the uncircumcised excluded from the covenant-people); 'cut off' variously understood (excommunicated, cut off from the congregation and privileges of the covenant-people, or cut off by death, a divine judgment, or the penalty of the law, the exclusion from the covenant and its blessings, the severing of the neglecter from the people of God); 'he hath broken my covenant' (the neglect of the sign a breaking of the covenant, to despise the token was to despise the covenant itself, the contempt of the sign contempt of God's gracious engagement, the seriousness of neglecting the covenant-sign, the warning to the covenant-people to keep the sign in faith) — Gill, Ellicott, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And the uncircumcised man child whose flesh of his foreskin is not circumcised, that soul shall be cut off from his people; he hath broken my covenant
the penalty of being cut off for neglecting circumcision
cut off - excommunication or death, exclusion from the covenant
the neglect of the sign a breaking of the covenant

schools.summary: Sarai renamed Sarah: 'and God said unto Abraham, As for Sarai thy wife, thou shalt not call her name Sarai, but Sarah shall her name be' — God changing Sarai's name to Sarah (the names close in form, both relating to 'princess/noblewoman,' Sarah meaning 'princess,' perhaps 'princess of a multitude,' fitting the promise that nations and kings would come of her, v.16, the new name, like Abraham's, v.5, marking and sealing her share in the covenant promise); the change from Sarai to Sarah marking her new dignity ('Sarah,' a princess, the mother of nations and kings, v.16, the matriarch given a name befitting the promise, the wife included, by name, in the covenant blessing); the changing of Sarah's name (as Abraham's, v.5) marking that the covenant promise of the seed was through her (not through Hagar or another, but through Sarah, the wife, the matriarch of the promised seed, the new name sealing her place as the mother of the covenant-line, Isaac, v.19, Sarah included in the covenant and its promise) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott, Calvin.

themes: And God said unto Abraham, As for Sarai thy wife, thou shalt not call her name Sarai, but Sarah shall her name be
the changing of Sarai's name to Sarah
Sarah, 'princess,' the mother of nations and kings (v.16)
the covenant promise of the seed through Sarah, not another

schools.summary: Sarah blessed with a son: 'and I will bless her, and give thee a son also of her: yea, I will bless her, and she shall be a mother of nations; kings of people shall be of her' — God promising to bless Sarah and give Abraham a son by her (Isaac, v.19, the express promise that the seed would be through Sarah, not Hagar, the matriarch, barren and old, to bear the son of promise by God's blessing, the human impossibility met by the divine promise); 'she shall be a mother of nations; kings of people shall be of her' (Sarah the mother of nations and the ancestress of kings, as Abraham, vv.4-6, the matriarch of the covenant-line, through whom would come Israel and the kings, and the King, Christ, the dignity of Sarah in the covenant, the promise fulfilled in Isaac and his seed); the repeated 'I will bless her' marking the certainty and fullness of the blessing on Sarah (the once-barren, now-honored matriarch, the mother of the promised seed, of nations, and of kings, the covenant blessing resting on Sarah as on Abraham) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And I will bless her, and give thee a son also of her... and she shall be a mother of nations; kings of people shall be of her
the promise to bless Sarah with a son and make her a mother of nations
the son of promise through Sarah (Isaac), not Hagar
the matriarch of nations and kings (and the King, Christ)

schools.summary: Clarke and many (the laughter of joy and faith): 'then Abraham fell upon his face, and laughed, and said in his heart, Shall a child be born unto him that is an hundred years old? and shall Sarah, that is ninety years old, bear?' — Abraham, falling on his face in worship, laughing for joy and wonder at the greatness of the promise (he believed, Romans 4:19-20 testifying that Abraham 'staggered not at the promise through unbelief, but was strong in faith... being fully persuaded,' his laughter the rejoicing of faith, cf. John 8:56, 'Abraham rejoiced to see my day,' the holy joy and admiration of the believing patriarch, not the doubt of unbelief); Abraham's words ('Shall a child be born unto him that is a hundred years old?') expressing the wonder of the thing — the natural impossibility against which the promise stood, yet the wonder the marvel of faith, not the objection of unbelief, Abraham, considering the impossibility, yet believing God who quickens the dead, Romans 4:17-21; Abraham's laugh (the laugh of joyful faith) distinguished from Sarah's laugh, 18:12, the laugh of incredulous wonder, which was reproved, Isaac, 'laughter,' 21:6, named for the joy.

A second reading takes the laughter as mixed with a momentary wonder or weakness — a natural astonishment at the seeming impossibility (a hundred-year-old man and a ninety-year-old woman bearing a child), expressing both faith and a touch of human marveling, though not the culpable unbelief of which Sarah's laugh (18:12) is more nearly reproved; the laughter divides between the joy/faith reading (dominant, Romans 4:19-20) and a wonder/weakness reading, with the contrast to Sarah's reproved laugh (18:12) noted on either view.

debates.question: Was Abraham's laughter the laughter of joy/faith or of doubt/weakness?

sides.position: The laughter of joy and faith - Abraham rejoicing and strong in faith, fully persuaded (the dominant reading, Romans 4:19-20; John 8:56)
A laughter mixed with momentary wonder or human weakness at the seeming impossibility (though not the culpable unbelief of Sarah's laugh, 18:12)

themes: Then Abraham fell upon his face, and laughed, and said in his heart, Shall a child be born unto him that is an hundred years old? and shall Sarah, that is ninety years old, bear?
Abraham's laughter at the promise (the crux of its meaning)
the laughter of joy and faith (Romans 4:19-20; John 8:56) vs a laughter mixed with wonder/weakness
distinguished from Sarah's reproved laugh (18:12)

schools.summary: Abraham's plea for Ishmael: 'and Abraham said unto God, O that Ishmael might live before thee!' — Abraham, in his joy and his love for his son Ishmael (then thirteen), pleading that Ishmael might live before God (that he might share in the covenant blessing, or be the heir, the patriarch's affection for Ishmael, the prayer that Ishmael might not be passed over); Abraham's plea springing from his fatherly love for Ishmael (perhaps a fear that the promise of a new son meant Ishmael's exclusion, the patriarch desiring that Ishmael, his firstborn, might live and be blessed before God, the natural affection of the father, brought to God in prayer); Abraham's prayer for Ishmael to be answered — God blessing Ishmael (v.20), though the covenant would be established with Isaac (v.21, the patriarch's affection met with God's gracious response, the love for Ishmael not unregarded, yet the covenant-line through Isaac) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Abraham said unto God, O that Ishmael might live before thee!
Abraham's plea for Ishmael
the patriarch's fatherly affection for his firstborn, brought to God in prayer
the prayer answered in God's blessing of Ishmael (v.20), though the covenant be with Isaac

schools.summary: The promise of Isaac: 'and God said, Sarah thy wife shall bear thee a son indeed; and thou shalt call his name Isaac: and I will establish my covenant with him for an everlasting covenant, and with his seed after him' — God affirming that Sarah herself (not Hagar) would bear Abraham a son ('indeed,' in truth, certainly, marking the certainty against the impossibility, v.17, the son of promise to be born of Sarah, the express confirming of the promise); the son to be named Isaac ('he laughs/laughter,' the name commemorating Abraham's joyful laugh, v.17, at the promise, and Sarah's, 18:12; 21:6, the son of laughter, of joy, the name given by God before the birth); 'I will establish my covenant with him' (the covenant, and the line of promise, established with Isaac, not Ishmael, Isaac the son of the covenant, through whom the promised seed, and the Messiah, would come, Romans 9:7; Galatians 4:28, the children of promise, the covenant-line fixed in Isaac, the everlasting covenant with Isaac and his seed) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And God said, Sarah thy wife shall bear thee a son indeed; and thou shalt call his name Isaac: and I will establish my covenant with him for an everlasting covenant
the promise of Isaac, the son of the covenant
the son of promise born of Sarah, named Isaac ('laughter')
the covenant-line established with Isaac, not Ishmael (Romans 9:7)

schools.summary: The blessing of Ishmael: 'and as for Ishmael, I have heard thee: Behold, I have blessed him, and will make him fruitful, and will multiply him exceedingly; twelve princes shall he beget, and I will make him a great nation' — God answering Abraham's plea (v.18) for Ishmael (the prayer of the father heard, God's gracious regard to Abraham's affection, Ishmael not unblessed, though not the covenant-heir); God blessing Ishmael — making him fruitful and multiplying him exceedingly (the temporal blessing on Ishmael, a numerous posterity, the Arab peoples, 16:10; 25:12-18, the kindness of God to Ishmael for Abraham's sake); 'twelve princes shall he beget, and I will make him a great nation' (Ishmael begetting twelve princes, the twelve sons/tribes, 25:12-16, and becoming a great nation, the Arab peoples, the temporal greatness of Ishmael, yet, v.21, the covenant with Isaac, the blessing on Ishmael great, but distinct from the covenant-line) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And as for Ishmael, I have heard thee... twelve princes shall he beget, and I will make him a great nation
the blessing of Ishmael in answer to Abraham's prayer
the temporal blessing - a numerous posterity, twelve princes, a great nation (25:12-16)
Ishmael blessed, yet the covenant with Isaac (v.21)

schools.summary: The covenant with Isaac: 'but my covenant will I establish with Isaac, which Sarah shall bear unto thee at this set time in the next year' — God fixing the covenant (and the line of promise) with Isaac, not Ishmael (the 'but' marking the distinction — Ishmael blessed, v.20, but the covenant with Isaac, the covenant-line, through which the promised seed and the Messiah would come, established in Isaac, Romans 9:7-9; Galatians 4:28); Isaac to be born of Sarah (the son of the wife, the matriarch of the promise, not of Hagar, but of Sarah, the covenant-seed through the appointed mother, the distinction between the son of the bondwoman, Ishmael, and the son of the free, Isaac, Galatians 4:22-31); 'at this set time in the next year' (God fixing the very time of Isaac's birth — at the set time, the following year, fulfilled, 21:2, the precise promise, the son of promise to be born at God's appointed time, the certainty and the timing of the fulfillment) — Gill, JFB, Wesley.

themes: But my covenant will I establish with Isaac, which Sarah shall bear unto thee at this set time in the next year
the covenant established with Isaac, born at the set time
the covenant-line through Isaac, not Ishmael (Romans 9:7-9; Galatians 4:28)
Isaac the son of Sarah (the free), born at God's appointed time (21:2)

schools.summary: God's communion closes: 'and he left off talking with him, and God went up from Abraham' — God ending his communication and going up (ascending, withdrawing the visible manifestation) from Abraham (the close of the theophany and the covenant-revelation, vv.1-21, 'went up' marking the withdrawal of the divine appearance, the conclusion of God's gracious communing with the patriarch); God 'left off talking' (the communication ended, the covenant fully declared, the names changed, the sign appointed, Isaac promised, the close of the revelation, leaving Abraham to obey, v.23); 'God went up from Abraham' (the visible appearance ascending and withdrawing, as in other theophanies, 35:13, the manifestation ended, God, having revealed the covenant, departing, the patriarch left to carry out the command, the circumcision of his house, v.23, the close of the divine appearing) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And he left off talking with him, and God went up from Abraham
the close of God's communion with Abraham
the covenant fully declared, the visible manifestation withdrawn
the patriarch left to carry out the command (v.23)

schools.summary: Abraham's prompt obedience: 'and Abraham took Ishmael his son, and all that were born in his house, and all that were bought with his money, every male among the men of Abraham's house; and circumcised the flesh of their foreskin in the selfsame day, as God had said unto him' — Abraham circumcising all the males of his household (Ishmael, the home-born, the bought servants) as God commanded (vv.10-13, the patriarch's full, exact obedience to the covenant-command); Abraham doing it 'in the selfsame day, as God had said unto him' (without delay, the very day God commanded, the prompt, immediate obedience of faith, the patriarch not deferring the hard command, the circumcision of himself, aged 99, and his whole house, but obeying at once, the readiness of Abraham's faith); Abraham's immediate, complete obedience — circumcising himself and his whole household the same day — the obedience of faith (he receiving the sign of the covenant promptly and fully, the pattern of ready obedience to God's command, the faith that obeys at once and entirely) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Abraham took Ishmael his son... and circumcised the flesh of their foreskin in the selfsame day, as God had said unto him
Abraham's prompt obedience in circumcising his house
the patriarch's immediate, complete obedience to the covenant-command
the readiness of faith, obeying at once and entirely

schools.summary: Abraham's age at circumcision: 'and Abraham was ninety years old and nine, when he was circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin' — Abraham receiving the covenant-sign at the age of ninety-nine (the patriarch, advanced in years, obeying the command and being circumcised, the dating of his circumcision, the same day God commanded, v.23); Abraham ninety-nine at his circumcision (the same year as the covenant, v.1, the late reception of the sign, long after his justification by faith, 15:6, twenty-four years before, so that, as Paul argues, Romans 4:10-11, Abraham was justified by faith while uncircumcised, and circumcision was the seal of a righteousness he already had); Abraham's circumcision at ninety-nine, long after his justification (15:6), the ground of Paul's argument (Romans 4:10-12) that righteousness is by faith, not by circumcision — Abraham being justified while yet uncircumcised, the father of believing Gentiles as well as Jews, the chronology serving the doctrine of justification by faith — Gill.

themes: And Abraham was ninety years old and nine, when he was circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin
Abraham's age at his circumcision
the sign received long after his justification by faith (15:6)
the chronology grounding Paul's argument for justification by faith (Romans 4:10-12)

schools.summary: Ishmael's age at circumcision: 'and Ishmael his son was thirteen years old, when he was circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin' — Ishmael thirteen when he was circumcised with the household (v.23, the son born thirteen years before, 16:16, now receiving the sign, the dating of Ishmael's circumcision); Ishmael thirteen (the Arabs, his descendants, said to circumcise at thirteen, after the example of Ishmael, the son circumcised at thirteen, with Abraham, 99, and the household, the age noted); Ishmael, with Abraham and all the males of the house (vv.23, 27), receiving the covenant-sign (the whole household circumcised together, Ishmael, though not the covenant-heir, v.21, yet circumcised as a member of Abraham's house, the sign borne by all the males) — Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And Ishmael his son was thirteen years old, when he was circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin
Ishmael's age at his circumcision
the son circumcised at thirteen with the household (16:16; v.23)
Ishmael circumcised as a member of Abraham's house, though not the covenant-heir

schools.summary: Abraham and Ishmael circumcised the same day: 'in the selfsame day was Abraham circumcised, and Ishmael his son' — Abraham and Ishmael (and the whole household, v.27) circumcised on the very day God commanded (v.23, the prompt, same-day obedience, the patriarch and his son receiving the sign together without delay, the immediate fulfillment of the command); it done 'in the selfsame day' (the very day of the command, v.23, the readiness of Abraham's obedience, no delay in the hard and humbling command, the faith that obeys at once); 'Abraham... and Ishmael his son' (the father and son circumcised together, the household head and his firstborn receiving the sign on the same day, the covenant-sign borne by Abraham, 99, and Ishmael, 13, alike, the patriarch leading his house in obedience) — Gill, Ellicott.

themes: In the selfsame day was Abraham circumcised, and Ishmael his son
Abraham and Ishmael circumcised the same day
the prompt, same-day obedience to the command (v.23)
the patriarch leading his house in obedience

schools.summary: The whole household circumcised: 'and all the men of his house, born in the house, and bought with money of the stranger, were circumcised with him' — all the males of Abraham's household (the home-born servants and the bought slaves) circumcised with Abraham (the complete obedience to the command, vv.12-13, the whole household brought into the covenant by the sign, not a male left uncircumcised); both the home-born and the purchased servants (even those not of Abraham's blood) circumcised (the whole household of faith included, the sign embracing all the males of the house, natural seed and servants alike); 'were circumcised with him' (the whole household circumcised together with Abraham, the patriarch and all his males receiving the sign, the complete, exact fulfillment of God's command, vv.10-13, the covenant-people, in Abraham's house, marked with the sign, the obedience of faith carried out fully) — Gill.

themes: And all the men of his house, born in the house, and bought with money of the stranger, were circumcised with him
the whole household circumcised with Abraham
both home-born and bought servants included in the covenant-sign
the patriarch's full, exact obedience to God's command
